posts.title: How I Turned My Great-Great-Grandfather's Immigration Story Into a Real Book
posts.summary: The journey of digitizing, editing, and printing a family memoir - from OCR nightmares to Christmas morning.
posts.body:
## Jorgen and His Times

A year or two ago, while I was at my parents' house, my dad walked into the room carrying a stack of papers clipped together. He told me it was a story his grandma had written about her father immigrating to Canada from Denmark.
I didn't know this existed until that moment.

He mentioned that he had read it once a long time ago and that he should probably read it again. He also told me this was the only copy of the book that he knew about.

Shortly after that conversation, I had a little idea: I wanted to turn it into a real book for him.

Fast forward about a year, and Christmas 2025 was approaching. I decided I would finally put the book together as a Christmas gift.

The first step was making a plan.

I needed to find the stack of papers and get them scanned. Since they had been written on a typewriter, I would have to properly reformat everything to create a digital version of the book. Then I'd need to figure out where I could have it printed and bound into an actual book.

By this point, I wasn't living with my parents anymore, so I asked my mom to find the stack of papers for me. Luckily, she works at a school and was able to scan them into a digital PDF without much trouble. It just took some time to work through all 100-plus pages.

Once she emailed me the PDF, I ran into my first major challenge.

The pages had been typed on a typewriter, and there was quite a bit of dirt and wear from when they had originally been photocopied. When I ran the document through an OCR tool (which basically converts a scanned PDF into editable text), it introduced a ton of errors. There were missing characters, extra characters, punctuation mistakes, and countless little formatting issues.

This became the most time-intensive part of the project.

I read through the entire book line by line, correcting every error I found. There wasn't really any shortcut. It was a completely manual process. It took me a good two or three weeks to finish, but once I was done, I finally had a clean digital copy of the manuscript.

There were also a few handwritten notes my grandma had left for my Dad, my uncle and a few other family members, so I was sure to include those too.

Next, I needed to figure out how to get it printed.

There was a custom bookbinder fairly close to my parents' house, so I stopped in after visiting them one day to ask about pricing.

After going back and forth on a few options, the total cost for printing the pages and professionally binding a single copy came to around $250.

As much as I would have loved to have the book custom-bound in beautiful leather by a local business, it was a lot more expensive than I had expected. I also hoped to make more than one copy, so I started looking into other options.
Eventually, I came across an online printing service called Lulu. They specialized in custom book printing, and honestly, the process was incredibly straightforward. All I needed to provide was a cover.

I have a bit of a background in graphic design, so I designed a simple cover for the book, putting my personal creative touch on the family heirloom. Then, I just had to upload the PDF, pay the printing fees, and I placed the order.

By this point, I was cutting it a little close to Christmas, but thankfully, the books arrived just in time. They were also affordable enough that I was able to order a second copy for my uncle, since we were all getting together for Christmas dinner with his family.

At that Christmas dinner, I gave both my dad and my uncle a copy of Jorgen and His Times.
They absolutely loved it.

In fact, they appreciated it so much that we ended up ordering another nine copies to share with the rest of the family.
It was an incredibly rewarding experience to put the time and effort into creating something so meaningful for our family, something that could continue to be reprinted and shared as our family grows.

Reading the story was equally meaningful. It gave me a much deeper appreciation for the hardships my great-great-grandfather and his family had endured when they immigrated to Canada and the difficult lives they lived. It's by no means a particularly happy story, but it is an inspiring one. Learning about my family's history has been an incredibly humbling experience.

I've included a link on the Studio page (https://www.gavinmakesstuff.com/studio/project.html?id=jorgen-and-his-times) to the PDF version of Jorgen and His Times if you'd like to read it for yourself.

seo.title: How I Turned My Great-Great-Grandfather's Immigration Story Into a Real Book

posts.title: How I Built a Personal Job Search Tool to Get Through a Career Change
posts.summary: How a career change job search led to building a personal job search tool, a fit scoring system that gives an honest read on whether a posting is worth your time.
posts.body:
## The problem with switching industries mid career

Changing industries partway through a career sounds straightforward until you are actually doing it. I have several years in technology and a recently earned professional certification, and I am working toward moving into a different sector entirely. On paper, a lot of my background should transfer. In practice, postings often bury a dealbreaker a few paragraphs down: several years of direct industry experience, a professional designation I do not hold, or a salary floor below what is workable.

Reading that closely, for every single posting, across multiple job boards, several times a week, is
exhausting in a way that is hard to explain until you have done it. It is not the rejection that wears a
person down. It is the repetition.

## Why existing job boards do not solve this

Job board filters are built around keywords and categories, not actual fit. A posting titled Project Coordinator can require either no industry experience or close to a decade of it, and the filters cannot tell the difference. I needed something that could read a posting the way a person would, checking the requirements against my actual background, but do it in seconds instead of minutes, consistently, for as many postings as I wanted to evaluate.

## Building a personal job search tool

The result is Scout, a small dashboard that runs on my own computer. I paste in a job posting and it
returns a structured assessment: a fit score out of ten, the reasoning behind that score, the practical
details of the role, and a set of resume keywords pulled directly from the listing.

Ease of use was the priority from the start. The entire workflow is built around pasting text and clicking one button, with no extra steps standing between finding a posting and getting a clear read on it. The most important design decision beyond that was making the scoring strict rather than encouraging. A job posting fit score only has value if it is willing to say a role is a poor match when it actually is. The scoring logic specifically checks for the gaps that matter most in a career change: years of required industry experience, professional designations, and salary thresholds. When those do not line up, the score reflects that clearly, with a specific explanation of what is missing.

## What a typical search session looks like

Postings get pasted in as they are found, sometimes one at a time, sometimes several at once. Within about thirty seconds each one comes back with a score out of ten, a short explanation, the salary and seniority level, whether the role is remote, hybrid, or on site, an estimated commute distance, the benefits mentioned in the listing, and a short summary of the employer's public reputation. Underneath that sits a set of resume and cover letter keywords, already sorted into categories, ready to drop into an application.

Promising roles get saved and starred if they stand out. Applied ones get marked and moved into a
separate tracker, where I can log the date I applied, whether a follow up has been sent, a contact name and email or LinkedIn profile, a direct link to the posting, and any notes I want to keep. Over a few weeks that turns into an organized, searchable record of an entire search, instead of a scattered mix of browser tabs, bookmarks, and half-remembered listings.

## What has changed

The biggest shift has not been speed, although it is faster. It has been confidence in the decision itself.
Instead of wondering whether a posting is being assessed too harshly or too generously, there is now a consistent, written rationale for every one I consider. That makes it much easier to apply quickly to
genuinely strong matches and move on from weak ones without the nagging feeling of having missed
something.

The resume keyword extraction has turned out to be just as valuable as the scoring. Tailoring a resume and cover letter for each application is one of those tasks everyone knows they should do and almost nobody does consistently, because it takes time. Having the relevant terms already pulled out and sorted removes most of that friction.

## Building your own version

For anyone in a similar position, switching industries, applying to a high volume of roles, or simply tired of reading the same kind of posting repeatedly, the core approach is straightforward to replicate. Define clear personal criteria such as industry, salary floor, required experience, and what is and is not a dealbreaker, then build a consistent system that checks new postings against those criteria honestly rather than optimistically. The specific tools used matter far less than being precise about what a good fit actually means for you.

Try it here (if you're my friend and I gave you the password): https://www.gavinmakesstuff.com/scout/
